s the waters fail from the sea nd the flood decayeth and drieth up o man lieth down and riseth not ill the heavens be no more they shall not awake or be raised out of their sleep
 that thou wouldest hide me in the grave hat thou wouldest keep me secret until thy wrath be past hat thou wouldest appoint me a set time and remember me
f a man die shall he live again
ll the days of my appointed time will  wait ill my change come
hou shalt call and  will answer thee hou wilt have a desire to the work of thine hands
or now thou numberest my steps ost thou not watch over my sin
y transgression is sealed up in a bag nd thou sewest up mine iniquity
nd surely the mountain falling cometh to nought nd the rock is removed out of his place
he waters wear the stones hou washest away the things which grow out of the dust of the earth nd thou destroyest the hope of man
hou prevailest for ever against him and he passeth hou changest his countenance and sendest him away
is sons come to honour and he knoweth it not nd they are brought low but he perceiveth it not of them
ut his flesh upon him shall have pain nd his soul within him shall mourn
hen answered liphaz the emanite and said hould a wise man utter vain knowledge nd fill his belly with the east wind
hould he reason with unprofitable talk
r with speeches wherewith he can do no good
ea thou castest off fear nd restrainest prayer before od
or thy mouth uttereth thine iniquity nd thou choosest the tongue of the crafty
hine own mouth condemneth thee and not  ea thine own lips testify against thee
rt thou the first man that was born
r wast thou made before the hills
ast thou heard the secret of od
nd dost thou restrain wisdom to thyself
hat knowest thou that we know not
hat understandest thou which is not in us
ith us are both the grayheaded and very aged men uch elder than thy father
re the consolations of od small with thee
s there any secret thing with thee
hy doth thine heart carry thee away
nd what do thy eyes wink at hat thou turnest thy spirit against od nd lettest such words go out of thy mouth
hat is man that he should be clean
nd he which is born of a woman that he should be righteous
ehold he putteth no trust in his saints ea the heavens are not clean in his sight
ow much more abominable and filthy is man hich drinketh iniquity like water
 will shew thee hear me nd that which  have seen  will declare hich wise men have toldrom their fathers and have not hid it nto whom alone the earth was given nd no stranger passed among them
he wicked man travaileth with pain all his days nd the number of years is hidden to the oppressor
 dreadful sound is in his ears n prosperity the destroyer shall come upon him
e believeth not that he shall return out of darkness nd he is waited for of the sword
e wandereth abroad for bread saying here is it
e knoweth that the day of darkness is ready at his hand
rouble and anguish shall make him afraid hey shall prevail against him as a king ready to the battle
or he stretcheth out his hand against od nd strengtheneth himself against the lmighty
e runneth upon him even on his neck pon the thick bosses of his bucklers ecause he covereth his face with his fatness nd maketh collops of fat on his flanks
nd he dwelleth in desolate cities nd in houses which no man inhabiteth hich are ready to become heaps
e shall not be rich neither shall his substance continue either shall he prolong the perfection thereof upon the earth
e shall not depart out of darkness he flame shall dry up his branches nd by the breath of his mouth shall he go away
et not him that is deceived trust in vanity or vanity shall be his recompence
t shall be accomplished before his time nd his branch shall not be green
e shall shake off his unripe grape as the vine nd shall cast off his flower as the olive
or the congregation of hypocrites shall be desolate nd fire shall consume the tabernacles of bribery
hey conceive mischief and bring forth vanity nd their belly prepareth deceit
hen ob answered and said  have heard many such things iserable comforters are ye all
hall vain words have an end
r what emboldeneth thee that thou answerest
 also could speak as ye do f your soul were in my souls stead  could heap up words against you nd shake mine head at you
ut  would strengthen you with my mouth nd the moving of my lips should asswage your grief
hough  speak my grief is not asswaged nd though  forbear what am  eased
ut now he hath made me weary hou hast made desolate all my company
nd thou hast filled me with wrinkles which is a witness against me nd my leanness rising up in me beareth witness to my face
e teareth me in his wrath who hateth me e gnasheth upon me with his teeth ine enemy sharpeneth his eyes upon me
hey have gaped upon me with their mouth hey have smitten me upon the cheek reproachfully hey have gathered themselves together against me
od hath delivered me to the ungodly nd turned me over into the hands of the wicked
 was at ease but he hath broken me asunder e hath also taken me by my neck and shaken me to pieces nd set me up for his mark
is archers compass me round about e cleaveth my reins asunder and doth not spare e poureth out my gall upon the ground
e breaketh me with breach upon breach e runneth upon me like a giant
 have sewed sackcloth upon my skin nd defiled my horn in the dust
y face is foul with weeping nd on my eyelids is the shadow of death ot for any injustice in mine hands lso my prayer is pure
 earth cover not thou my blood nd let my cry have no place
lso now behold my witness is in heaven nd my record is on high
y friends scorn me but mine eye poureth out tears unto od
 that one might plead for a man with od s a man pleadeth for his neighbour
hen a few years are come hen  shall go the way whence  shall not return
y breath is corrupt my days are extinct he graves are ready for me
re there not mockers with me
nd doth not mine eye continue in their provocation
ay down now put me in a surety with thee ho is he that will strike hands with me
or thou hast hid their heart from understanding herefore shalt thou not exalt them
e that speaketh flattery to his friends ven the eyes of his children shall fail
e hath made me also a byword of the people nd aforetime  was as a tabret
ine eye also is dim by reason of sorrow nd all my members are as a shadow
pright men shall be astonied at this nd the innocent shall stir up himself against the hypocrite
he righteous also shall hold on his way nd he that hath clean hands shall be stronger and stronger
ut as for you all do ye return and come now or  cannot find one wise man among you
y days are past my purposes are broken off ven the thoughts of my heart
hey change the night into day he light is short because of darkness
f  wait the grave is mine house  have made my bed in the darkness
 have said to corruption hou art my father o the worm hou art my mother and my sister
nd where is now my hope
s for my hope who shall see it
hey shall go down to the bars of the pit hen our rest together is in the dust
hen answered ildad the huhite and said ow long will it be ere ye make an end of words
ark and afterwards we will speak
herefore are we counted as beasts nd reputed vile in your sight
e teareth himself in his anger hall the earth be forsaken for thee
nd shall the rock be removed out of his place
ea the light of the wicked shall be put out nd the spark of his fire shall not shine
he light shall be dark in his tabernacle nd his candle shall be put out with him
he steps of his strength shall be straitened nd his own counsel shall cast him down
or he is cast into a net by his own feet nd he walketh upon a snare
he gin shall take him by the heel nd the robber shall prevail against him
he snare is laid for him in the ground nd a trap for him in the way
errors shall make him afraid on every side nd shall drive him to his feet
is strength shall be hungerbitten nd destruction shall be ready at his side
t shall devour the strength of his skin ven the firstborn of death shall devour his strength
is confidence shall be rooted out of his tabernacle nd it shall bring him to the king of terrors
t shall dwell in his tabernacle because it is none of his rimstone shall be scattered upon his habitation
is roots shall be dried up beneath nd above shall his branch be cut off
is remembrance shall perish from the earth nd he shall have no name in the street
e shall be driven from light into darkness nd chased out of the world
e shall neither have son nor nephew among his people or any remaining in his dwellings
hey that come after him shall be astonied at his day s they that went before were affrighted
urely such are the dwellings of the wicked nd this is the place of him that knoweth not od
hen ob answered and said ow long will ye vex my soul nd break me in pieces with words
hese ten times have ye reproached me e are not ashamed that ye make yourselves strange to me
nd be it indeed that  have erred ine error remaineth with myself
f indeed ye will magnify yourselves against me nd plead against me my reproach now now that od hath overthrown me nd hath compassed me with his net
ehold  cry out of wrong but  am not heard  cry aloud but there is no judgment
e hath fenced up my way that  cannot pass nd he hath set darkness in my paths
e hath stripped me of my glory nd taken the crown from my head
e hath destroyed me on every side and  am gone nd mine hope hath he removed like a tree
e hath also kindled his wrath against me nd he counteth me unto him as one of his enemies
is troops come together nd raise up their way against me nd encamp round about my tabernacle
e hath put my brethren far from me nd mine acquaintance are verily estranged from me
y kinsfolk have failed and my familiar friends have forgotten me
hey that dwell in mine house nd my maids count me for a stranger  am an alien in their sight
 called my servant and he gave me no answer  intreated him with my mouth
y breath is strange to my wife hough  intreated for the childrens sake of mine own body
ea young children despised me  arose and they spake against me
ll my inward friends abhorred me nd they whom  loved are turned against me
y bone cleaveth to my skin and to my flesh nd  am escaped with the skin of my teeth
ave pity upon me have pity upon me  ye my friends or the hand of od hath touched me
hy do ye persecute me as od nd are not satisfied with my flesh
h that my words were now written
h that they were printed in a book
hat they were graven with an iron pen and leadn the rock for ever
or  know that my redeemer liveth nd that he shall stand at the latter day upon the earth nd though after my skin worms destroy this body et in my flesh shall  see od hom  shall see for myself nd mine eyes shall behold and not another hough my reins be consumed within me
ut ye should say hy persecute we him eeing the root of the matter is found in me
e ye afraid of the sword or wrath bringeth the punishments of the sword hat ye may know there is a judgment
hen answered ophar the aamathite and said herefore do my thoughts cause me to answer nd for this  make haste
 have heard the check of my reproach nd the spirit of my understanding causeth me to answer
nowest thou not this of old ince man was placed upon earth hat the triumphing of the wicked is short nd the joy of the hypocrite but for a moment
hough his excellency mount up to the heavens nd his head reach unto the clouds et he shall perish for ever like his own dung hey which have seen him shall say here is he
e shall fly away as a dream and shall not be found ea he shall be chased away as a vision of the night
he eye also which saw him shall see him no more either shall his place any more behold him
is children shall seek to please the poor nd his hands shall restore their goods
is bones are full of the sin of his youth hich shall lie down with him in the dust
hough wickedness be sweet in his mouth hough he hide it under his tongue hough he spare it and forsake it not ut keep it still within his mouth et his meat in his bowels is turned t is the gall of asps within him
e hath swallowed down riches and he shall vomit them up again od shall cast them out of his belly
e shall suck the poison of asps he vipers tongue shall slay him
e shall not see the rivers he floods the brooks of honey and butter
hat which he laboured for shall he restore nd shall not swallow it down ccording to his substance shall the restitution be and he shall not rejoice therein
ecause he hath oppressed and hath forsaken the poor ecause he hath violently taken away an house which he builded not urely he shall not feel quietness in his belly e shall not save of that which he desired
here shall none of his meat be left herefore shall no man look for his goods
n the fulness of his sufficiency he shall be in straits very hand of the wicked shall come upon him
hen he is about to fill his belly od shall cast the fury of his wrath upon him nd shall rain it upon him while he is eating
e shall flee from the iron weapon nd the bow of steel shall strike him through
t is drawn and cometh out of the body ea the glittering sword cometh out of his gall errors are upon him
ll darkness shall be hid in his secret places  fire not blown shall consume him t shall go ill with him that is left in his tabernacle
he heaven shall reveal his iniquity nd the earth shall rise up against him
he increase of his house shall depart nd his goods shall flow away in the day of his wrath
his is the portion of a wicked man from od nd the heritage appointed unto him by od
ut ob answered and said ear diligently my speech nd let this be your consolations
uffer me that  may speak nd after that  have spoken mock on
s for me is my complaint to man
nd if it were so why should not my spirit be troubled
ark me and be astonished nd lay your hand upon your mouth
ven when  remember  am afraid nd trembling taketh hold on my flesh
herefore do the wicked live ecome old yea are mighty in power
heir seed is established in their sight with them nd their offspring before their eyes
heir houses are safe from fear either is the rod of od upon them
heir bull gendereth and faileth not heir cow calveth and casteth not her calf
hey send forth their little ones like a flock nd their children dance
hey take the timbrel and harp nd rejoice at the sound of the organ
hey spend their days in wealth nd in a moment go down to the grave
herefore they say unto od epart from us or we desire not the knowledge of thy ways
hat is the lmighty that we should serve him
nd what profit should we have if we pray unto him
o their good is not in their hand he counsel of the wicked is far from me
ow oft is the candle of the wicked put out
nd how oft cometh their destruction upon them
od distributeth sorrows in his anger
hey are as stubble before the wind nd as chaff that the storm carrieth away
od layeth up his iniquity for his children e rewardeth him and he shall know it
is eyes shall see his destruction nd he shall drink of the wrath of the lmighty
or what pleasure hath he in his house after him hen the number of his months is cut off in the midst
hall any teach od knowledge
eeing he judgeth those that are high
ne dieth in his full strength eing wholly at ease and quiet
is breasts are full of milk nd his bones are moistened with marrow
nd another dieth in the bitterness of his soul nd never eateth with pleasure
hey shall lie down alike in the dust nd the worms shall cover them
ehold  know your thoughts nd the devices which ye wrongfully imagine against me
or ye say here is the house of the prince
nd where are the dwelling places of the wicked
ave ye not asked them that go by the way
nd do ye not know their tokens hat the wicked is reserved to the day of destruction
hey shall be brought forth to the day of wrath
ho shall declare his way to his face
nd who shall repay him what he hath done
et shall he be brought to the grave nd shall remain in the tomb
he clods of the valley shall be sweet unto him nd every man shall draw after him s there are innumerable before him
ow then comfort ye me in vain eeing in your answers there remaineth falsehood
hen liphaz the emanite answered and said an a man be profitable unto od s he that is wise may be profitable unto himself
s it any pleasure to the lmighty that thou art righteous
r is it gain to him that thou makest thy ways perfect
ill he reprove thee for fear of thee
ill he enter with thee into judgment
s not thy wickedness great
nd thine iniquities infinite
or thou hast taken a pledge from thy brother for nought nd stripped the naked of their clothing
hou hast not given water to the weary to drink nd thou hast withholden bread from the hungry
ut as for the mighty man he had the earth nd the honourable man dwelt in it
hou hast sent widows away empty nd the arms of the fatherless have been broken
herefore snares are round about thee nd sudden fear troubleth thee r darkness that thou canst not see nd abundance of waters cover thee
s not od in the height of heaven
nd behold the height of the stars how high they are
nd thou sayest ow doth od know
an he judge through the dark cloud
hick clouds are a covering to him that he seeth not nd he walketh in the circuit of heaven
ast thou marked the old wayhich wicked men have trodden
hich were cut down out of time hose foundation was overflown with a flood hich said unto od epart from us nd what can the lmighty do for them
et he filled their houses with good things ut the counsel of the wicked is far from me
he righteous see it and are glad nd the innocent laugh them to scorn
hereas our substance is not cut down ut the remnant of them the fire consumeth
cquaint now thyself with him and be at peace hereby good shall come unto thee
eceive  pray thee the law from his mouth nd lay up his words in thine heart
f thou return to the lmighty thou shalt be built up hou shalt put away iniquity far from thy tabernacles
hen shalt thou lay up gold as dust nd the gold of phir as the stones of the brooks
ea the lmighty shall be thy defence nd thou shalt have plenty of silver
or then shalt thou have thy delight in the lmighty nd shalt lift up thy face unto od
hou shalt make thy prayer unto him and he shall hear thee nd thou shalt pay thy vows
hou shalt also decree a thing and it shall be established unto thee nd the light shall shine upon thy ways
hen men are cast down then thou shalt say there is lifting up nd he shall save the humble person
e shall deliver the island of the innocent nd it is delivered by the pureness of thine hands
hen ob answered and said ven to day is my complaint bitter y stroke is heavier than my groaning
h that  knew where  might find him
hat  might come even to his seat
 would order my cause before him nd fill my mouth with arguments
 would know the words which he would answer me nd understand what he would say unto me
ill he plead against me with his great power
o but he would put strength in me
here the righteous might dispute with him o should  be delivered for ever from my judge
ehold  go forward but he is not there nd backward but  cannot perceive him n the left hand where he doth work but  cannot behold him e hideth himself on the right hand that  cannot see him ut he knoweth the way that  take hen he hath tried me  shall come forth as gold
y foot hath held his steps is way have  kept and not declined
either have  gone back from the commandment of his lips  have esteemed the words of his mouth more than my necessary food
ut he is in one mind and who can turn him
nd what his soul desireth even that he doeth
or he performeth the thing that is appointed for me nd many such things are with him
herefore am  troubled at his presence hen  consider  am afraid of him
or od maketh my heart soft nd the lmighty troubleth me ecause  was not cut off before the darkness either hath he covered the darkness from my face
hy seeing times are not hidden from the lmighty o they that know him not see his days
ome remove the landmarks hey violently take away flocks and feed thereof
hey drive away the ass of the fatherless hey take the widows ox for a pledge
hey turn the needy out of the way he poor of the earth hide themselves together
ehold as wild asses in the desert o they forth to their work rising betimes for a prey he wilderness yieldeth food for them and for their children
hey reap every one his corn in the field nd they gather the vintage of the wicked
hey cause the naked to lodge without clothing hat they have no covering in the cold
hey are wet with the showers of the mountains nd embrace the rock for want of a shelter
hey pluck the fatherless from the breast nd take a pledge of the poor
hey cause him to go naked without clothing nd they take away the sheaf from the hungry hich make oil within their walls nd tread their winepresses and suffer thirst
en groan from out of the city nd the soul of the wounded crieth out et od layeth not folly to them
hey are of those that rebel against the light hey know not the ways thereof or abide in the paths thereof
he murderer rising with the light killeth the poor and needy nd in the night is as a thief
he eye also of the adulterer waiteth for the twilight aying o eye shall see me nd disguiseth his face
n the dark they dig through houses hich they had marked for themselves in the daytime hey know not the light
or the morning is to them even as the shadow of death f one know them they are in the terrors of the shadow of death
e is swift as the waters heir portion is cursed in the earth e beholdeth not the way of the vineyards
rought and heat consume the snow waters o doth the grave those which have sinned
he womb shall forget him the worm shall feed sweetly on him e shall be no more remembered nd wickedness shall be broken as a tree
e evil entreateth the barren that beareth not nd doeth not good to the widow
e draweth also the mighty with his power e riseth up and no man is sure of life
hough it be given him to be in safety whereon he resteth et his eyes are upon their ways
hey are exalted for a little while but are gonend brought low they are taken out of the way as all other nd cut off as the tops of the ears of corn
nd if it be not so now who will make me a liar nd make my speech nothing worth
hen answered ildad the huhite and said ominion and fear are with him e maketh peace in his high places
s there any number of his armies
nd upon whom doth not his light arise
ow then can man be justified with od
r how can he be clean that is born of a woman
ehold even to the moon and it shineth not ea the stars are not pure in his sight
ow much less man that is a worm
nd the son of man which is a worm
ut ob answered and said ow hast thou helped him that is without power
ow savest thou the arm that hath no strength
ow hast thou counselled him that hath no wisdom
nd how hast thou plentifully declared the thing as it is
o whom hast thou uttered words
nd whose spirit came from thee
ead things are formedrom under the waters and the inhabitants thereof
ell is naked before him nd destruction hath no covering
e stretcheth out the north over the empty place nd hangeth the earth upon nothing
e bindeth up the waters in his thick clouds nd the cloud is not rent under them
e holdeth back the face of his throne nd spreadeth his cloud upon it
e hath compassed the waters with bounds ntil the day and night come to an end
he pillars of heaven tremblend are astonished at his reproof
e divideth the sea with his power nd by his understanding he smiteth through the proud
y his spirit he hath garnished the heavens is hand hath formed the crooked serpent
o these are parts of his ways ut how little a portion is heard of him
ut the thunder of his power who can understand
oreover ob continued his parable and said s od liveth who hath taken away my judgment nd the lmighty who hath vexed my soul ll the while my breath is in me nd the spirit of od is in my nostrils y lips shall not speak wickedness or my tongue utter deceit
od forbid that  should justify you ill  die  will not remove mine integrity from me
y righteousness  hold fast and will not let it go y heart shall not reproach me so long as  live
et mine enemy be as the wicked nd he that riseth up against me as the unrighteous
or what is the hope of the hypocrite though he hath gained hen od taketh away his soul
ill od hear his cryhen trouble cometh upon him
ill he delight himself in the lmighty
ill he always call upon od
 will teach you by the hand of od hat which is with the lmighty will  not conceal
ehold all ye yourselves have seen it hy then are ye thus altogether vain
his is the portion of a wicked man with od nd the heritage of oppressors which they shall receive of the lmighty
f his children be multiplied it is for the sword nd his offspring shall not be satisfied with bread
hose that remain of him shall be buried in death nd his widows shall not weep
hough he heap up silver as the dust nd prepare raiment as the clay e may prepare it but the just shall put it on nd the innocent shall divide the silver
e buildeth his house as a moth nd as a booth that the keeper maketh
he rich man shall lie down but he shall not be gathered e openeth his eyes and he is not
errors take hold on him as waters  tempest stealeth him away in the night
he east wind carrieth him away and he departeth nd as a storm hurleth him out of his place
or od shall cast upon him and not spare e would fain flee out of his hand
en shall clap their hands at him nd shall hiss him out of his place
urely there is a vein for the silver nd a place for gold where they fine it
ron is taken out of the earth nd brass is molten out of the stone
e setteth an end to darkness nd searcheth out all perfection he stones of darkness and the shadow of death
he flood breaketh out from the inhabitant ven the waters forgotten of the foot hey are dried up they are gone away from men
s for the earth out of it cometh bread nd under it is turned up as it were fire
he stones of it are the place of sapphires nd it hath dust of gold
here is a path which no fowl knoweth nd which the vultures eye hath not seen he lions whelps have not trodden it or the fierce lion passed by it
e putteth forth his hand upon the rock e overturneth the mountains by the roots
e cutteth out rivers among the rocks nd his eye seeth every precious thing
e bindeth the floods from overflowing nd the thing that is hid bringeth he forth to light
ut where shall wisdom be found
nd where is the place of understanding
an knoweth not the price thereof either is it found in the land of the living
he depth saith t is not in me nd the sea saith t is not with me
t cannot be gotten for gold either shall silver be weighed for the price thereof
t cannot be valued with the gold of phir ith the precious onyx or the sapphire
he gold and the crystal cannot equal it nd the exchange of it shall not be for jewels of fine gold
o mention shall be made of coral or of pearls or the price of wisdom is above rubies
he topaz of thiopia shall not equal it either shall it be valued with pure gold
hence then cometh wisdom
nd where is the place of understanding
eeing it is hid from the eyes of all living nd kept close from the fowls of the air
estruction and death say e have heard the fame thereof with our ears
od understandeth the way thereof nd he knoweth the place thereof
or he looketh to the ends of the earth nd seeth under the whole heaven o make the weight for the winds nd he weigheth the waters by measure
hen he made a decree for the rain nd a way for the lightning of the thunder hen did he see it and declare it e prepared it yea and searched it out
nd unto man he said ehold the fear of the ord that is wisdom nd to depart from evil is understanding
oreover ob continued his parable and said h that  were as in months past s in the days when od preserved me hen his candle shined upon my head nd when by his light  walked through darkness s  was in the days of my youth hen the secret of od was upon my tabernacle hen the lmighty was yet with me hen my children were about me hen  washed my steps with butter nd the rock poured me out rivers of oil hen  went out to the gate through the city hen  prepared my seat in the street
he young men saw me and hid themselves nd the aged arose and stood up
he princes refrained talking nd laid their hand on their mouth
he nobles held their peace nd their tongue cleaved to the roof of their mouth
hen the ear heard me then it blessed me nd when the eye saw me it gave witness to me ecause  delivered the poor that cried nd the fatherless and him that had none to help him
he blessing of him that was ready to perish came upon me nd  caused the widows heart to sing for joy
 put on righteousness and it clothed me y judgment was as a robe and a diadem
 was eyes to the blind nd feet was  to the lame
 was a father to the poor nd the cause which  knew not  searched out
nd  brake the jaws of the wicked nd plucked the spoil out of his teeth
hen  said  shall die in my nest nd  shall multiply my days as the sand
y root was spread out by the waters nd the dew lay all night upon my branch
y glory was fresh in me nd my bow was renewed in my hand
nto me men gave ear and waited nd kept silence at my counsel
fter my words they spake not again nd my speech dropped upon them
nd they waited for me as for the rain nd they opened their mouth wide as for the latter rain
f  laughed on them they believed it not nd the light of my countenance they cast not down
 chose out their way and sat chief nd dwelt as a king in the army s one that comforteth the mourners
ut now they that are younger than  have me in derision hose fathers  would have disdained to have set with the dogs of my flock
ea whereto might the strength of their hands profit me n whom old age was perished
or want and famine they were solitary leeing into the wilderness in former time desolate and waste
ho cut up mallows by the bushes nd juniper roots for their meat
hey were driven forth from among men hey cried after them as after a thief o dwell in the cliffs of the valleys in caves of the earth and in the rocks
mong the bushes they brayed nder the nettles they were gathered together
hey were children of fools yea children of base men hey were viler than the earth
nd now am  their song ea  am their byword
hey abhor me they flee far from me nd spare not to spit in my face
ecause he hath loosed my cord and afflicted me hey have also let loose the bridle before me
pon my right hand rise the youth hey push away my feet nd they raise up against me the ways of their destruction
hey mar my path hey set forward my calamity hey have no helper
hey came upon me as a wide breaking in of waters n the desolation they rolled themselves upon me
errors are turned upon me hey pursue my soul as the wind nd my welfare passeth away as a cloud
nd now my soul is poured out upon me he days of affliction have taken hold upon me
y bones are pierced in me in the night season nd my sinews take no rest
y the great force of my disease is my garment changed t bindeth me about as the collar of my coat
e hath cast me into the mire nd  am become like dust and ashes
 cry unto thee and thou dost not hear me  stand up and thou regardest me not
hou art become cruel to me ith thy strong hand thou opposest thyself against me
hou liftest me up to the wind thou causest me to ride upon it nd dissolvest my substance
or  know that thou wilt bring me to death nd to the house appointed for all living
owbeit he will not stretch out his hand to the grave hough they cry in his destruction
id not  weep for him that was in trouble
as not my soul grieved for the poor
hen  looked for good then evil came unto me nd when  waited for light there came darkness
y bowels boiled and rested not he days of affliction prevented me
 went mourning without the sun  stood up and  cried in the congregation
 am a brother to dragons nd a companion to owls
y skin is black upon me nd my bones are burned with heat
y harp also is turned to mourning nd my organ into the voice of them that weep
 made a covenant with mine eyes hy then should  think upon a maid
or what portion of od is there from above
nd what inheritance of the lmighty from on high
s not destruction to the wicked
nd a strange punishment to the workers of iniquity
oth not he see my ways nd count all my steps
f  have walked with vanity r if my foot hath hasted to deceit et me be weighed in an even balance hat od may know mine integrity
f my step hath turned out of the way nd mine heart walked after mine eyes nd if any blot hath cleaved to mine hands hen let me sow and let another eat ea let my offspring be rooted out
f mine heart have been deceived by a woman r if  have laid wait at my neighbours door hen let my wife grind unto another nd let others bow down upon her
or this is an heinous crime ea it is an iniquity to be punished by the judges
or it is a fire that consumeth to destruction nd would root out all mine increase
f  did despise the cause of my manservant or of my maidservant hen they contended with me hat then shall  do when od riseth up
nd when he visiteth what shall  answer him
id not he that made me in the womb make him
nd did not one fashion us in the womb
f  have withheld the poor from their desire r have caused the eyes of the widow to fail r have eaten my morsel myself alone nd the fatherless hath not eaten thereof or from my youth he was brought up with me as with a father nd  have guided her from my mothers womb f  have seen any perish for want of clothing r any poor without covering f his loins have not blessed me nd if he were not warmed with the fleece of my sheep f  have lifted up my hand against the fatherless hen  saw my help in the gate hen let mine arm fall from my shoulder blade nd mine arm be broken from the bone
or destruction from od was a terror to me nd by reason of his highness  could not endure
f  have made gold my hope r have said to the fine gold hou art my confidence f  rejoiced because my wealth was great nd because mine hand had gotten much f  beheld the sun when it shined r the moon walking in brightness nd my heart hath been secretly enticed r my mouth hath kissed my hand his also were an iniquity to be punished by the judge or  should have denied the od that is above
f  rejoiced at the destruction of him that hated me r lifted up myself when evil found him either have  suffered my mouth to siny wishing a curse to his soul
f the men of my tabernacle said not h that we had of his flesh we cannot be satisfied
he stranger did not lodge in the street ut  opened my doors to the traveller
f  covered my transgressions as dam y hiding mine iniquity in my bosom id  fear a great multitude r did the contempt of families terrify me hat  kept silence and went not out of the door
h that one would hear me
ehold my desire is that the lmighty would answer me nd that mine adversary had written a book
urely  would take it upon my shoulder nd bind it as a crown to me
 would declare unto him the number of my steps s a prince would  go near unto him
f my land cry against me r that the furrows likewise thereof complain f  have eaten the fruits thereof without money r have caused the owners thereof to lose their life et thistles grow instead of wheat nd cockle instead of barley
he words of ob are ended
o these three men ceased to answer ob because he was righteous in his own eyes
hen was kindled the wrath of lihu the son of arachel the uzite of the kindred of am against ob was his wrath kindled because he justified himself rather than od
lso against his three friends was his wrath kindled because they had found no answer and yet had condemned ob
ow lihu had waited till ob had spoken because they were elder than he
hen lihu saw that there was no answer in the mouth of these three men then his wrath was kindled
nd lihu the son of arachel the uzite answered and said  am young and ye are very old herefore  was afraid and durst not shew you mine opinion
 said ays should speak nd multitude of years should teach wisdom
ut there is a spirit in man nd the inspiration of the lmighty giveth them understanding
reat men are not always wise either do the aged understand judgment
herefore  said earken to me  also will shew mine opinion
ehold  waited for your words  gave ear to your reasons hilst ye searched out what to say
ea  attended unto you nd behold there was none of you that convinced ob r that answered his words est ye should say e have found out wisdom od thrusteth him down not man
ow he hath not directed his words against me either will  answer him with your speeches
hey were amazed they answered no more hey left off speaking
hen  had waited for they spake not ut stood still and answered no more  said  will answer also my part  also will shew mine opinion
or  am full of matter he spirit within me constraineth me
ehold my belly is as wine which hath no vent t is ready to burst like new bottles
 will speak that  may be refreshed  will open my lips and answer
et me not  pray you accept any mans person either let me give flattering titles unto man
or  know not to give flattering titles n so doing my aker would soon take me away
herefore ob  pray thee hear my speeches nd hearken to all my words
ehold now  have opened my mouth y tongue hath spoken in my mouth
y words shall be of the uprightness of my heart nd my lips shall utter knowledge clearly
he pirit of od hath made me nd the breath of the lmighty hath given me life
f thou canst answer me et thy words in order before me stand up
ehold  am according to thy wish in ods stead  also am formed out of the clay
ehold my terror shall not make thee afraid either shall my hand be heavy upon thee
urely thou hast spoken in mine hearing nd  have heard the voice of thy words saying  am clean without transgression  am innocent neither is there iniquity in me
ehold he findeth occasions against me e counteth me for his enemy e putteth my feet in the stocks e marketh all my paths
ehold in this thou art not just  will answer thee that od is greater than man
hy dost thou strive against him
or he giveth not account of any of his matters
or od speaketh once ea twice yet man perceiveth it not
n a dream in a vision of the night hen deep sleep falleth upon men n slumberings upon the bed hen he openeth the ears of men nd sealeth their instruction hat he may withdraw man from his purpose nd hide pride from man
e keepeth back his soul from the pit nd his life from perishing by the sword
e is chastened also with pain upon his bed nd the multitude of his bones with strong pain o that his life abhorreth bread nd his soul dainty meat
is flesh is consumed away that it cannot be seen nd his bones that were not seen stick out
ea his soul draweth near unto the grave nd his life to the destroyers
f there be a messenger with him n interpreter one among a thousand o shew unto man his uprightness hen he is gracious unto him and saith eliver him from going down to the pit  have found a ransom
is flesh shall be fresher than a childs e shall return to the days of his youth e shall pray unto od and he will be favourable unto him nd he shall see his face with joy or he will render unto man his righteousness
e looketh upon men and if any say  have sinned and perverted that which was right nd it profited me not e will deliver his soul from going into the pit nd his life shall see the light
o all these things worketh odftentimes with man o bring back his soul from the pit o be enlightened with the light of the living
ark well  ob hearken unto me old thy peace and  will speak

et us choose to us judgment et us know among ourselves what is good

hould  lie against my right
y wound is incurable without transgression
hat man is like ob ho drinketh up scorning like water
hich goeth in company with the workers of iniquity nd walketh with wicked men
or he hath said t profiteth a man nothinghat he should delight himself with od
herefore hearken unto me ye men of understanding ar be it from od that he should do wickedness nd from the lmighty that he should commit iniquity
or the work of a man shall he render unto him nd cause every man to find according to his ways
ea surely od will not do wickedly either will the lmighty pervert judgment
ho hath given him a charge over the earth
r who hath disposed the whole world
f he set his heart upon man f he gather unto himself his spirit and his breath ll flesh shall perish together nd man shall turn again unto dust

hall even he that hateth right govern

ow much less to him that accepteth not the persons of princes or regardeth the rich more than the poor
or they all are the work of his hands
n a moment shall they die nd the people shall be troubled at midnight and pass away nd the mighty shall be taken away without hand
or his eyes are upon the ways of man nd he seeth all his goings
here is no darkness nor shadow of death here the workers of iniquity may hide themselves
or he will not lay upon man more than right hat he should enter into judgment with od
e shall break in pieces mighty men without number nd set others in their stead
herefore he knoweth their works nd he overturneth them in the night so that they are destroyed

e sealeth up the hand of every man hat all men may know his work
hen the beasts go into dens nd remain in their places
ut of the south cometh the whirlwind nd cold out of the north

r fill the appetite of the young lions hen they couch in their dens nd abide in the covert to lie in wait

hey bow themselves they bring forth their young ones hey cast out their sorrows
heir young ones are in good liking they grow up with corn hey go forth and return not unto them

e scorneth the multitude of the city either regardeth he the crying of the driver
he range of the mountains is his pasture nd he searcheth after every green thing

hich leaveth her eggs in the earth nd warmeth them in dust nd forgetteth that the foot may crush them r that the wild beast may break them

hat time she lifteth up herself on high he scorneth the horse and his rider

he proverbs of olomon the son of avid king of srael o know wisdom and instruction o perceive the words of understanding o receive the instruction of wisdom ustice and judgment and equity o give subtilty to the simple o the young man knowledge and discretion

orsake the foolish and live nd go in the way of understanding

eprove not a scorner lest he hate thee ebuke a wise man and he will love thee

ut he knoweth not that the dead are there nd that her guests are in the depths of hell
he proverbs of olomon
 wise son maketh a glad father ut a foolish son is the heaviness of his mother
reasures of wickedness profit nothing ut righteousness delivereth from death
he  will not suffer the soul of the righteous to famish ut he casteth away the substance of the wicked
e becometh poor that dealeth with a slack hand ut the hand of the diligent maketh rich
e that gathereth in summer is a wise son ut he that sleepeth in harvest is a son that causeth shame
lessings are upon the head of the just ut violence covereth the mouth of the wicked
he memory of the just is blessed ut the name of the wicked shall rot
he wise in heart will receive commandments ut a prating fool shall fall
e that walketh uprightly walketh surely ut he that perverteth his ways shall be known
e that winketh with the eye causeth sorrow ut a prating fool shall fall
he mouth of a righteous man is a well of life ut violence covereth the mouth of the wicked
atred stirreth up strifes ut love covereth all sins
n the lips of him that hath understanding wisdom is found ut a rod is for the back of him that is void of understanding
ise men lay up knowledge ut the mouth of the foolish is near destruction
he rich mans wealth is his strong city he destruction of the poor is their poverty
he labour of the righteous tendeth to life he fruit of the wicked to sin
e is in the way of life that keepeth instruction ut he that refuseth reproof erreth
e that hideth hatred with lying lips nd he that uttereth a slander is a fool
n the multitude of words there wanteth not sin ut he that refraineth his lips is wise
he tongue of the just is as choice silver he heart of the wicked is little worth
he lips of the righteous feed many ut fools die for want of wisdom
he blessing of the  it maketh rich nd he addeth no sorrow with it

s vinegar to the teeth and as smoke to the eyes o is the sluggard to them that send him
he fear of the  prolongeth days ut the years of the wicked shall be shortened
he hope of the righteous shall be gladness ut the expectation of the wicked shall perish
he way of the  is strength to the upright ut destruction shall be to the workers of iniquity
he righteous shall never be removed ut the wicked shall not inhabit the earth
he mouth of the just bringeth forth wisdom ut the froward tongue shall be cut out

hen pride cometh then cometh shame ut with the lowly is wisdom
he integrity of the upright shall guide them ut the perverseness of transgressors shall destroy them
iches profit not in the day of wrath ut righteousness delivereth from death
he righteousness of the perfect shall direct his way ut the wicked shall fall by his own wickedness
he righteousness of the upright shall deliver them ut transgressors shall be taken in their own naughtiness
hen a wicked man dieth his expectation shall perish nd the hope of unjust men perisheth
he righteous is delivered out of trouble nd the wicked cometh in his stead
n hypocrite with his mouth destroyeth his neighbour ut through knowledge shall the just be delivered
hen it goeth well with the righteous the city rejoiceth nd when the wicked perish there is shouting

he words of the reacher the son of avid king in erusalem
anity of vanities saith the reacher vanity of vanities all is vanity
hat profit hath a man of all his labour which he taketh under the sun
ne generation passeth away and another generation cometh but the earth abideth for ever
he sun also ariseth and the sun goeth down and hasteth to his place where he arose
he wind goeth toward the south and turneth about unto the north it whirleth about continually and the wind returneth again according to his circuits
ll the rivers run into the sea yet the sea is not full unto the place from whence the rivers come thither they return again
ll things are full of labour man cannot utter it the eye is not satisfied with seeing nor the ear filled with hearing
he thing that hath been it is that which shall be and that which is done is that which shall be done and there is no new thing under the sun
s there any thing whereof it may be said ee this is new it hath been already of old time which was before us
here is no remembrance of former things neither shall there be any remembrance of things that are to come with those that shall come after
 the reacher was king over srael in erusalem
nd  gave my heart to seek and search out by wisdom concerning all things that are done under heaven this sore travail hath od given to the sons of man to be exercised therewith
 have seen all the works that are done under the sun and behold all is vanity and vexation of spirit
hat which is crooked cannot be made straight and that which is wanting cannot be numbered
 communed with mine own heart saying o  am come to great estate and have gotten more wisdom than all they that have been before me in erusalem yea my heart had great experience of wisdom and knowledge
nd  gave my heart to know wisdom and to know madness and folly  perceived that this also is vexation of spirit
or in much wisdom is much grief and he that increaseth knowledge increaseth sorrow
 said in mine heart o to now  will prove thee with mirth therefore enjoy pleasure and behold this also is vanity
 said of laughter t is mad and of mirth hat doeth it
 sought in mine heart to give myself unto wine yet acquainting mine heart with wisdom and to lay hold on folly till  might see what was that good for the sons of men which they should do under the heaven all the days of their life
 made me great works  builded me houses  planted me vineyards  made me gardens and orchards and  planted trees in them of all kind of fruits  made me pools of water to water therewith the wood that bringeth forth trees  got me servants and maidens and had servants born in my house also  had great possessions of great and small cattle above all that were in erusalem before me  gathered me also silver and gold and the peculiar treasure of kings and of the provinces  gat me men singers and women singers and the delights of the sons of men as musical instruments and that of all sorts
o  was great and increased more than all that were before me in erusalem also my wisdom remained with me
nd whatsoever mine eyes desired  kept not from them  withheld not my heart from any joy for my heart rejoiced in all my labour and this was my portion of all my labour
hen  looked on all the works that my hands had wrought and on the labour that  had laboured to do and behold all was vanity and vexation of spirit and there was no profit under the sun
nd  turned myself to behold wisdom and madness and folly for what can the man do that cometh after the king even that which hath been already done
hen  saw that wisdom excelleth folly as far as light excelleth darkness
he wise mans eyes are in his head but the fool walketh in darkness and  myself perceived also that one event happeneth to them all
hen said  in my heart s it happeneth to the fool so it happeneth even to me and why was  then more wise
hen  said in my heart that this also is vanity
or there is no remembrance of the wise more than of the fool for ever seeing that which now is in the days to come shall all be forgotten
nd how dieth the wise man as the fool
herefore  hated life because the work that is wrought under the sun is grievous unto me for all is vanity and vexation of spirit
ea  hated all my labour which  had taken under the sun because  should leave it unto the man that shall be after me
nd who knoweth whether he shall be a wise man or a fool yet shall he have rule over all my labour wherein  have laboured and wherein  have shewed myself wise under the sun
his is also vanity
herefore  went about to cause my heart to despair of all the labour which  took under the sun
or there is a man whose labour is in wisdom and in knowledge and in equity yet to a man that hath not laboured therein shall he leave it for his portion
his also is vanity and a great evil
or what hath man of all his labour and of the vexation of his heart wherein he hath laboured under the sun
or all his days are sorrows and his travail grief yea his heart taketh not rest in the night
his is also vanity
here is nothing better for a man than that he should eat and drink and that he should make his soul enjoy good in his labour
his also  saw that it was from the hand of od
or who can eat or who else can hasten hereunto more than 
or od giveth to a man that is good in his sight wisdom and knowledge and joy but to the sinner he giveth travail to gather and to heap up that he may give to him that is good before od
his also is vanity and vexation of spirit
o every thing there is a season and a time to every purpose under the heaven a time to be born and a time to die a time to plant and a time to pluck up that which is planted a time to kill and a time to heal a time to break down and a time to build up a time to weep and a time to laugh a time to mourn and a time to dance a time to cast away stones and a time to gather stones together a time to embrace and a time to refrain from embracing a time to get and a time to lose a time to keep and a time to cast away a time to rend and a time to sew a time to keep silence and a time to speak a time to love and a time to hate a time of war and a time of peace
hat profit hath he that worketh in that wherein he laboureth
 have seen the travail which od hath given to the sons of men to be exercised in it
e hath made every thing beautiful in his time also he hath set the world in their heart so that no man can find out the work that od maketh from the beginning to the end
 know that there is no good in them but for a man to rejoice and to do good in his life
nd also that every man should eat and drink and enjoy the good of all his labour it is the gift of od
 know that whatsoever od doeth it shall be for ever nothing can be put to it nor any thing taken from it and od doeth it that men should fear before him
hat which hath been is now and that which is to be hath already been and od requireth that which is past
nd moreover  saw under the sun the place of judgment that wickedness was there and the place of righteousness that iniquity was there
 said in mine heart od shall judge the righteous and the wicked for there is a time there for every purpose and for every work
 said in mine heart concerning the estate of the sons of men that od might manifest them and that they might see that they themselves are beasts
or that which befalleth the sons of men befalleth beasts even one thing befalleth them as the one dieth so dieth the other yea they have all one breath so that a man hath no preeminence above a beast for all is vanity
ll go unto one place all are of the dust and all turn to dust again
ho knoweth the spirit of man that goeth upward and the spirit of the beast that goeth downward to the earth
herefore  perceive that there is nothing better than that a man should rejoice in his own works for that is his portion for who shall bring him to see what shall be after him
o  returned and considered all the oppressions that are done under the sun and behold the tears of such as were oppressed and they had no comforter and on the side of their oppressors there was power but they had no comforter
herefore  praised the dead which are already dead more than the living which are yet alive
ea better is he than both they which hath not yet been who hath not seen the evil work that is done under the sun
gain  considered all travail and every right work that for this a man is envied of his neighbour
his is also vanity and vexation of spirit
he fool foldeth his hands together and eateth his own flesh
etter is an handful with quietness than both the hands full with travail and vexation of spirit
hen  returned and  saw vanity under the sun
here is one alone and there is not a second yea he hath neither child nor brother yet is there no end of all his labour neither is his eye satisfied with riches neither saith he or whom do  labour and bereave my soul of good
his is also vanity yea it is a sore travail
wo are better than one because they have a good reward for their labour
or if they fall the one will lift up his fellow but woe to him that is alone when he falleth for he hath not another to help him up
gain if two lie together then they have heat but how can one be warm alone
nd if one prevail against him two shall withstand him and a threefold cord is not quickly broken
etter is a poor and a wise child than an old and foolish king who will no more be admonished
or out of prison he cometh to reign whereas also he that is born in his kingdom becometh poor
 considered all the living which walk under the sun with the second child that shall stand up in his stead
here is no end of all the people even of all that have been before them they also that come after shall not rejoice in him
urely this also is vanity and vexation of spirit
eep thy foot when thou goest to the house of od and be more ready to hear than to give the sacrifice of fools for they consider not that they do evil
e not rash with thy mouth and let not thine heart be hasty to utter any thing before od for od is in heaven and thou upon earth therefore let thy words be few
or a dream cometh through the multitude of business and a fools voice is known by multitude of words
hen thou vowest a vow unto od defer not to pay it for he hath no pleasure in fools pay that which thou hast vowed
etter is it that thou shouldest not vow than that thou shouldest vow and not pay
uffer not thy mouth to cause thy flesh to sin neither say thou before the angel that it was an error wherefore should od be angry at thy voice and destroy the work of thine hands
or in the multitude of dreams and many words there are also divers vanities but fear thou od
f thou seest the oppression of the poor and violent perverting of judgment and justice in a province marvel not at the matter for he that is higher than the highest regardeth and there be higher than they
oreover the profit of the earth is for all the king himself is served by the field
e that loveth silver shall not be satisfied with silver nor he that loveth abundance with increase this is also vanity
hen goods increase they are increased that eat them and what good is there to the owners thereof saving the beholding of them with their eyes
he sleep of a labouring man is sweet whether he eat little or much but the abundance of the rich will not suffer him to sleep
here is a sore evil which  have seen under the sun namely riches kept for the owners thereof to their hurt
ut those riches perish by evil travail and he begetteth a son and there is nothing in his hand
s he came forth of his mothers womb naked shall he return to go as he came and shall take nothing of his labour which he may carry away in his hand
nd this also is a sore evil that in all points as he came so shall he go and what profit hath he that hath laboured for the wind
ll his days also he eateth in darkness and he hath much sorrow and wrath with his sickness
ehold that which  have seen it is good and comely for one to eat and to drink and to enjoy the good of all his labour that he taketh under the sun all the days of his life which od giveth him for it is his portion
very man also to whom od hath given riches and wealth and hath given him power to eat thereof and to take his portion and to rejoice in his labour this is the gift of od
or he shall not much remember the days of his life because od answereth him in the joy of his heart
here is an evil which  have seen under the sun and it is common among men a man to whom od hath given riches wealth and honour so that he wanteth nothing for his soul of all that he desireth yet od giveth him not power to eat thereof but a stranger eateth it this is vanity and it is an evil disease
f a man beget an hundred children and live many years so that the days of his years be many and his soul be not filled with good and also that he have no burial  say that an untimely birth is better than he
or he cometh in with vanity and departeth in darkness and his name shall be covered with darkness
oreover he hath not seen the sun nor known any thing this hath more rest than the other
ea though he live a thousand years twice told yet hath he seen no good do not all go to one place
ll the labour of man is for his mouth and yet the appetite is not filled
or what hath the wise more than the fool what hath the poor that knoweth to walk before the living
etter is the sight of the eyes than the wandering of the desire this is also vanity and vexation of spirit
hat which hath been is named already and it is known that it is man neither may he contend with him that is mightier than he
eeing there be many things that increase vanity what is man the better
or who knoweth what is good for man in this life all the days of his vain life which he spendeth as a shadow for who can tell a man what shall be after him under the sun
 good name is better than precious ointment and the day of death than the day of ones birth
t is better to go to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting for that is the end of all men and the living will lay it to his heart
orrow is better than laughter for by the sadness of the countenance the heart is made better
he heart of the wise is in the house of mourning but the heart of fools is in the house of mirth
t is better to hear the rebuke of the wise than for a man to hear the song of fools
or as the crackling of thorns under a pot so is the laughter of the fool this also is vanity
urely oppression maketh a wise man mad and a gift destroyeth the heart
etter is the end of a thing than the beginning thereof and the patient in spirit is better than the proud in spirit
e not hasty in thy spirit to be angry for anger resteth in the bosom of fools
ay not thou hat is the cause that the former days were better than these for thou dost not enquire wisely concerning this
isdom is good with an inheritance and by it there is profit to them that see the sun
or wisdom is a defence and money is a defence but the excellency of knowledge is that wisdom giveth life to them that have it
onsider the work of od for who can make that straight which he hath made crooked
n the day of prosperity be joyful but in the day of adversity consider od also hath set the one over against the other to the end that man should find nothing after him
ll things have  seen in the days of my vanity there is a just man that perisheth in his righteousness and there is a wicked man that prolongeth his life in his wickedness
e not righteous over much neither make thyself over wise why shouldest thou destroy thyself
e not over much wicked neither be thou foolish why shouldest thou die before thy time
t is good that thou shouldest take hold of this yea also from this withdraw not thine hand for he that feareth od shall come forth of them all
isdom strengtheneth the wise more than ten mighty men which are in the city
or there is not a just man upon earth that doeth good and sinneth not

hat which is far off and exceeding deep who can find it out
 applied mine heart to know and to search and to seek out wisdom and the reason of things and to know the wickedness of folly even of foolishness and madness and  find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is snares and nets and her hands as bands whoso pleaseth od shall escape from her but the sinner shall be taken by her

o this only have  found that od hath made man upright but they have sought out many inventions
ho is as the wise man and who knoweth the interpretation of a thing a mans wisdom maketh his face to shine and the boldness of his face shall be changed
 counsel thee to keep the kings commandment and that in regard of the oath of od

here is no man that hath power over the spirit to retain the spirit neither hath he power in the day of death and there is no discharge in that war neither shall wickedness deliver those that are given to it

ecause sentence against an evil work is not executed speedily therefore the heart of the sons of men is fully set in them to do evil
hough a sinner do evil an hundred times and his days be prolonged yet surely  know that it shall be well with them that fear od which fear before him but it shall not be well with the wicked neither shall he prolong his days which are as a shadow because he feareth not before od
here is a vanity which is done upon the earth that there be just men unto whom it happeneth according to the work of the wicked again there be wicked men to whom it happeneth according to the work of the righteous  said that this also is vanity
hen  commended mirth because a man hath no better thing under the sun than to eat and to drink and to be merry for that shall abide with him of his labour the days of his life which od giveth him under the sun
hen  applied mine heart to know wisdom and to see the business that is done upon the earth for also there is that neither day nor night seeth sleep with his eyes then  beheld all the work of od that a man cannot find out the work that is done under the sun because though a man labour to seek it out yet he shall not find it yea farther though a wise man think to know it yet shall he not be able to find it
or all this  considered in my heart even to declare all this that the righteous and the wise and their works are in the hand of od no man knoweth either love or hatred by all that is before them
ll things come alike to all there is one event to the righteous and to the wicked to the good and to the clean and to the unclean to him that sacrificeth and to him that sacrificeth not as is the good so is the sinner and he that sweareth as he that feareth an oath
his is an evil among all things that are done under the sun that there is one event unto all yea also the heart of the sons of men is full of evil and madness is in their heart while they live and after that they go to the dead
or to him that is joined to all the living there is hope for a living dog is better than a dead lion
or the living know that they shall die but the dead know not any thing neither have they any more a reward for the memory of them is forgotten

 returned and saw under the sun that the race is not to the swift nor the battle to the strong neither yet bread to the wise nor yet riches to men of understanding nor yet favour to men of skill but time and chance happeneth to them all

nd when ye spread forth your hands  will hide mine eyes from you yea when ye make many prayers  will not hear your hands are full of blood

he crown of pride the drunkards of phraim shall be trodden under feet and the glorious beauty which is on the head of the fat valley shall be a fading flower and as the hasty fruit before the summer which when he that looketh upon it seeth while it is yet in his hand he eateth it up

oth the plowman plow all day to sow doth he open and break the clods of his ground

 sat not in the assembly of the mockers nor rejoiced  sat alone because of thy hand for thou hast filled me with indignation
hy is my pain perpetual and my wound incurable which refuseth to be healed wilt thou be altogether unto me as a liar and as waters that fail
herefore thus saith the  f thou return then will  bring thee again and thou shalt stand before me and if thou take forth the precious from the vile thou shalt be as my mouth let them return unto thee but return not thou unto them

nd  will deliver thee out of the hand of the wicked and  will redeem thee out of the hand of the terrible
he word of the  came also unto me saying hou shalt not take thee a wife neither shalt thou have sons or daughters in this place
or thus saith the  concerning the sons and concerning the daughters that are born in this place and concerning their mothers that bare them and concerning their fathers that begat them in this land they shall die of grievous deaths they shall not be lamented neither shall they be buried but they shall be as dung upon the face of the earth and they shall be consumed by the sword and by famine and their carcases shall be meat for the fowls of heaven and for the beasts of the earth
or thus saith the  nter not into the house of mourning neither go to lament nor bemoan them for  have taken away my peace from this people saith the  even lovingkindness and mercies
oth the great and the small shall die in this land they shall not be buried neither shall men lament for them nor cut themselves nor make themselves bald for them neither shall men tear themselves for them in mourning to comfort them for the dead neither shall men give them the cup of consolation to drink for their father or for their mother
hou shalt not also go into the house of feasting to sit with them to eat and to drink
or thus saith the  of hosts the od of srael ehold  will cause to cease out of this place in your eyes and in your days the voice of mirth and the voice of gladness the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride

hen shalt thou say unto them ecause your fathers have forsaken me saith the  and have walked after other gods and have served them and have worshipped them and have forsaken me and have not kept my law and ye have done worse than your fathers for behold ye walk every one after the imagination of his evil heart that they may not hearken unto me
herefore will  cast you out of this land into a land that ye know not neither ye nor your fathers and there shall ye serve other gods day and night where  will not shew you favour

ehold  will send for many fishers saith the  and they shall fish them and after will  send for many hunters and they shall hunt them from every mountain and from every hill and out of the holes of the rocks
or mine eyes are upon all their ways they are not hid from my face neither is their iniquity hid from mine eyes
nd first  will recompense their iniquity and their sin double because they have defiled my land they have filled mine inheritance with the carcases of their detestable and abominable things
